242.play: Lo, in this right hand, whose protection

243.play: Is most divinely vow'd upon the right

244.play: Of him it holds, stands young Plantagenet,

245.play: Son to the elder brother of this man,

246.play: And king o'er him and all that he enjoys:

247.play: For this down-trodden equity, we tread

248.play: In warlike march these greens before your town,

249.play: Being no further enemy to you

250.play: Than the constraint of hospitable zeal

251.play: In the relief of this oppressed child

253.play: To pay that duty which you truly owe

254.play: To that owes it, namely this young prince:

255.play: And then our arms, like to a muzzled bear,

256.play: Save in aspect, hath all offence seal'd up;

257.play: Our cannons' malice vainly shall be spent

258.play: Against the invulnerable clouds of heaven;

259.play: And with a blessed and unvex'd retire,

260.play: With unhack'd swords and helmets all unbruised,

261.play: We will bear home that lusty blood again

262.play: Which here we came to spout against your town,

263.play: And leave your children, wives and you in peace.

264.play: But if you fondly pass our proffer'd offer,

265.play: 'Tis not the roundure of your old-faced walls

266.play: Can hide you from our messengers of war,

267.play: Though all these English and their discipline

268.play: Were harbour'd in their rude circumference.

269.play: Then tell us, shall your city call us lord,

270.play: In that behalf which we have challenged it?

271.play: Or shall we give the signal to our rage

272.play: And stalk in blood to our possession?

273.play: In brief, we are the king of England's subjects:

274.play: For him, and in his right, we hold this town.

275.play: KING JOHN: Acknowledge then the king, and let me in.

276.play: That can we not; but he that proves the king,

277.play: To him will we prove loyal: till that time

278.play: Have we ramm'd up our gates against the world.

279.play: Doth not the crown of England prove the king?

280.play: And if not that, I bring you witnesses,

281.play: Twice fifteen thousand hearts of England's breed,--

283.play: KING JOHN: To verify our title with their lives.

284.play: KING PHILIP: As many and as well-born bloods as those,--

286.play: KING PHILIP: Stand in his face to contradict his claim.

287.play: First Citizen: Till you compound whose right is worthiest,

288.play: We for the worthiest hold the right from both.

289.play: KING JOHN: Then God forgive the sin of all those souls

291.play: Before the dew of evening fall, shall fleet,

292.play: In dreadful trial of our kingdom's king!

293.play: KING PHILIP: Amen, amen! Mount, chevaliers! to arms!

294.play: BASTARD: Saint George, that swinged the dragon, and e'er since

295.play: Sits on his horseback at mine hostess' door,

298.play: At your den, sirrah, with your lioness

299.play: I would set an ox-head to your lion's hide,

300.play: And make a monster of you.

302.play: BASTARD: O tremble, for you hear the lion roar.

303.play: KING JOHN: Up higher to the plain; where we'll set forth

304.play: In best appointment all our regiments.

305.play: Speed then, to take advantage of the field.

306.play: KING PHILIP: It shall be so; and at the other hill

307.play: Command the rest to stand. God and our right!

308.play: French Herald: You men of Angiers, open wide your gates,

309.play: And let young Arthur, Duke of Bretagne, in,

310.play: Who by the hand of France this day hath made

311.play: Much work for tears in many an English mother,

312.play: Whose sons lie scattered on the bleeding ground;

313.play: Many a widow's husband grovelling lies,

315.play: And victory, with little loss, doth play

316.play: Upon the dancing banners of the French,

317.play: Who are at hand, triumphantly display'd,

318.play: To enter conquerors and to proclaim

319.play: Arthur of Bretagne England's king and yours.

320.play: English Herald: Rejoice, you men of Angiers, ring your bells:

321.play: King John, your king and England's doth approach,

322.play: Commander of this hot malicious day:

323.play: Their armours, that march'd hence so silver-bright,

324.play: Hither return all gilt with Frenchmen's blood;

325.play: There stuck no plume in any English crest

326.play: That is removed by a staff of France;

327.play: Our colours do return in those same hands

328.play: That did display them when we first march'd forth;

329.play: And, like a troop of jolly huntsmen, come

330.play: Our lusty English, all with purpled hands,

331.play: Dyed in the dying slaughter of their foes:

332.play: Open your gates and gives the victors way.

333.play: First Citizen: Heralds, from off our towers we might behold,

334.play: From first to last, the onset and retire

335.play: Of both your armies; whose equality

336.play: By our best eyes cannot be censured:

337.play: Blood hath bought blood and blows have answered blows;

338.play: Strength match'd with strength, and power confronted power:

339.play: Both are alike; and both alike we like.

340.play: One must prove greatest: while they weigh so even,

341.play: We hold our town for neither, yet for both.

342.play: KING JOHN: France, hast thou yet more blood to cast away?

343.play: Say, shall the current of our right run on?

344.play: Whose passage, vex'd with thy impediment,

345.play: Shall leave his native channel and o'erswell

346.play: With course disturb'd even thy confining shores,

347.play: Unless thou let his silver water keep

348.play: A peaceful progress to the ocean.

349.play: KING PHILIP: England, thou hast not saved one drop of blood,

350.play: In this hot trial, more than we of France;

351.play: Rather, lost more. And by this hand I swear,

352.play: That sways the earth this climate overlooks,

353.play: Before we will lay down our just-borne arms,

354.play: We'll put thee down, 'gainst whom these arms we bear,

355.play: Or add a royal number to the dead,

356.play: Gracing the scroll that tells of this war's loss

357.play: With slaughter coupled to the name of kings.

358.play: BASTARD: Ha, majesty! how high thy glory towers,

359.play: When the rich blood of kings is set on fire!

360.play: O, now doth Death line his dead chaps with steel;

361.play: The swords of soldiers are his teeth, his fangs;

362.play: And now he feasts, mousing the flesh of men,

364.play: Why stand these royal fronts amazed thus?

365.play: Cry, 'havoc!' kings; back to the stained field,

366.play: You equal potents, fiery kindled spirits!

367.play: Then let confusion of one part confirm

368.play: The other's peace: till then, blows, blood and death!

369.play: KING JOHN: Whose party do the townsmen yet admit?

370.play: KING PHILIP: Speak, citizens, for England; who's your king?

371.play: First Citizen: The king of England; when we know the king.

372.play: Know him in us, that here hold up his right.

373.play: KING JOHN: In us, that are our own great deputy

374.play: And bear possession of our person here,

375.play: Lord of our presence, Angiers, and of you.

376.play: A greater power then we denies all this;

377.play: And till it be undoubted, we do lock

378.play: Our former scruple in our strong-barr'd gates;

379.play: King'd of our fears, until our fears, resolved,

380.play: Be by some certain king purged and deposed.

381.play: BASTARD: By heaven, these scroyles of Angiers flout you, kings,

382.play: And stand securely on their battlements,

383.play: As in a theatre, whence they gape and point

384.play: At your industrious scenes and acts of death.

385.play: Your royal presences be ruled by me:

386.play: Do like the mutines of Jerusalem,

387.play: Be friends awhile and both conjointly bend

388.play: Your sharpest deeds of malice on this town:

389.play: By east and west let France and England mount

390.play: Their battering cannon charged to the mouths,

391.play: Till their soul-fearing clamours have brawl'd down

392.play: The flinty ribs of this contemptuous city:

393.play: I'ld play incessantly upon these jades,

395.play: Leave them as naked as the vulgar air.

396.play: That done, dissever your united strengths,

397.play: And part your mingled colours once again;

398.play: Turn face to face and bloody point to point;

399.play: Then, in a moment, Fortune shall cull forth

400.play: Out of one side her happy minion,

401.play: To whom in favour she shall give the day,

402.play: And kiss him with a glorious victory.

403.play: How like you this wild counsel, mighty states?

404.play: Smacks it not something of the policy?

405.play: KING JOHN: Now, by the sky that hangs above our heads,

406.play: I like it well. France, shall we knit our powers

407.play: And lay this Angiers even to the ground;

408.play: Then after fight who shall be king of it?

409.play: An if thou hast the mettle of a king,

410.play: Being wronged as we are by this peevish town,

411.play: Turn thou the mouth of thy artillery,

412.play: As we will ours, against these saucy walls;

413.play: And when that we have dash'd them to the ground,

414.play: Why then defy each other and pell-mell

415.play: Make work upon ourselves, for heaven or hell.

416.play: KING PHILIP: Let it be so. Say, where will you assault?

417.play: KING JOHN: We from the west will send destruction

420.play: KING PHILIP: Our thunder from the south

421.play: Shall rain their drift of bullets on this town.

422.play: BASTARD: O prudent discipline! From north to south:

423.play: Austria and France shoot in each other's mouth:

424.play: I'll stir them to it. Come, away, away!

425.play: First Citizen: Hear us, great kings: vouchsafe awhile to stay,

426.play: And I shall show you peace and fair-faced league;

427.play: Win you this city without stroke or wound;

428.play: Rescue those breathing lives to die in beds,

429.play: That here come sacrifices for the field:

430.play: Persever not, but hear me, mighty kings.

431.play: KING JOHN: Speak on with favour; we are bent to hear.

432.play: That daughter there of Spain, the Lady Blanch,

433.play: Is niece to England: look upon the years

434.play: Of Lewis the Dauphin and that lovely maid:

435.play: If lusty love should go in quest of beauty,

436.play: Where should he find it fairer than in Blanch?

437.play: If zealous love should go in search of virtue,

438.play: Where should he find it purer than in Blanch?

439.play: If love ambitious sought a match of birth,

440.play: Whose veins bound richer blood than Lady Blanch?

441.play: Such as she is, in beauty, virtue, birth,

442.play: Is the young Dauphin every way complete:

443.play: If not complete of, say he is not she;

444.play: And she again wants nothing, to name want,

445.play: If want it be not that she is not he:

446.play: He is the half part of a blessed man,

447.play: Left to be finished by such as she;

448.play: And she a fair divided excellence,

449.play: Whose fulness of perfection lies in him.

450.play: O, two such silver currents, when they join,

451.play: Do glorify the banks that bound them in;

452.play: And two such shores to two such streams made one,

453.play: Two such controlling bounds shall you be, kings,

454.play: To these two princes, if you marry them.

455.play: This union shall do more than battery can

456.play: To our fast-closed gates; for at this match,

457.play: With swifter spleen than powder can enforce,

458.play: The mouth of passage shall we fling wide ope,

459.play: And give you entrance: but without this match,

460.play: The sea enraged is not half so deaf,

461.play: Lions more confident, mountains and rocks

462.play: More free from motion, no, not Death himself

463.play: In moral fury half so peremptory,

464.play: As we to keep this city.

466.play: That shakes the rotten carcass of old Death

467.play: Out of his rags! Here's a large mouth, indeed,

468.play: That spits forth death and mountains, rocks and seas,

469.play: Talks as familiarly of roaring lions

470.play: As maids of thirteen do of puppy-dogs!

471.play: What cannoneer begot this lusty blood?

472.play: He speaks plain cannon fire, and smoke and bounce;

473.play: He gives the bastinado with his tongue:

474.play: Our ears are cudgell'd; not a word of his

475.play: But buffets better than a fist of France:

476.play: Zounds! I was never so bethump'd with words

477.play: Since I first call'd my brother's father dad.

478.play: QUEEN ELINOR: Son, list to this conjunction, make this match;

479.play: Give with our niece a dowry large enough:

480.play: For by this knot thou shalt so surely tie

481.play: Thy now unsured assurance to the crown,

482.play: That yon green boy shall have no sun to ripe

483.play: The bloom that promiseth a mighty fruit.

484.play: I see a yielding in the looks of France;

485.play: Mark, how they whisper: urge them while their souls

487.play: Lest zeal, now melted by the windy breath

488.play: Of soft petitions, pity and remorse,

489.play: Cool and congeal again to what it was.

490.play: First Citizen: Why answer not the double majesties

491.play: This friendly treaty of our threaten'd town?

492.play: KING PHILIP: Speak England first, that hath been forward first

493.play: To speak unto this city: what say you?

494.play: KING JOHN: If that the Dauphin there, thy princely son,

495.play: Can in this book of beauty read 'I love,'

496.play: Her dowry shall weigh equal with a queen:

497.play: For Anjou and fair Touraine, Maine, Poictiers,

498.play: And all that we upon this side the sea,

499.play: Except this city now by us besieged,

500.play: Find liable to our crown and dignity,

501.play: Shall gild her bridal bed and make her rich

503.play: As she in beauty, education, blood,

504.play: Holds hand with any princess of the world.

505.play: KING PHILIP: What say'st thou, boy? look in the lady's face.

506.play: LEWIS: I do, my lord; and in her eye I find

507.play: A wonder, or a wondrous miracle,

508.play: The shadow of myself form'd in her eye:

509.play: Which being but the shadow of your son,

510.play: Becomes a sun and makes your son a shadow:

511.play: I do protest I never loved myself

512.play: Till now infixed I beheld myself

513.play: Drawn in the flattering table of her eye.

514.play: BASTARD: Drawn in the flattering table of her eye!

515.play: Hang'd in the frowning wrinkle of her brow!

516.play: And quarter'd in her heart! he doth espy

517.play: Himself love's traitor: this is pity now,

518.play: That hang'd and drawn and quartered, there should be

519.play: In such a love so vile a lout as he.

520.play: BLANCH: My uncle's will in this respect is mine:

521.play: If he see aught in you that makes him like,

522.play: That any thing he sees, which moves his liking,

523.play: I can with ease translate it to my will;

524.play: Or if you will, to speak more properly,

525.play: I will enforce it easily to my love.

526.play: Further I will not flatter you, my lord,

527.play: That all I see in you is worthy love,

528.play: Than this; that nothing do I see in you,

529.play: Though churlish thoughts themselves should be your judge,

530.play: That I can find should merit any hate.

531.play: KING JOHN: What say these young ones? What say you my niece?

532.play: That she is bound in honour still to do

533.play: What you in wisdom still vouchsafe to say.

534.play: KING JOHN: Speak then, prince Dauphin; can you love this lady?

535.play: LEWIS: Nay, ask me if I can refrain from love;

536.play: For I do love her most unfeignedly.

537.play: KING JOHN: Then do I give Volquessen, Touraine, Maine,

538.play: Poictiers and Anjou, these five provinces,

539.play: With her to thee; and this addition more,

540.play: Full thirty thousand marks of English coin.

541.play: Philip of France, if thou be pleased withal,

542.play: Command thy son and daughter to join hands.

543.play: KING PHILIP: It likes us well; young princes, close your hands.

544.play: AUSTRIA: And your lips too; for I am well assured

545.play: That I did so when I was first assured.

546.play: KING PHILIP: Now, citizens of Angiers, ope your gates,

547.play: Let in that amity which you have made;

548.play: For at Saint Mary's chapel presently

549.play: The rites of marriage shall be solemnized.

550.play: Is not the Lady Constance in this troop?

551.play: I know she is not, for this match made up

552.play: Her presence would have interrupted much:

553.play: Where is she and her son? tell me, who knows.

554.play: LEWIS: She is sad and passionate at your highness' tent.

555.play: KING PHILIP: And, by my faith, this league that we have made

556.play: Will give her sadness very little cure.

557.play: Brother of England, how may we content

558.play: This widow lady? In her right we came;

559.play: Which we, God knows, have turn'd another way,

561.play: KING JOHN: We will heal up all;

562.play: For we'll create young Arthur Duke of Bretagne

563.play: And Earl of Richmond; and this rich fair town

564.play: We make him lord of. Call the Lady Constance;

565.play: Some speedy messenger bid her repair

566.play: To our solemnity: I trust we shall,

567.play: If not fill up the measure of her will,

568.play: Yet in some measure satisfy her so

569.play: That we shall stop her exclamation.

570.play: Go we, as well as haste will suffer us,

571.play: To this unlook'd for, unprepared pomp.

1.play: CONSTANCE: Gone to be married! gone to swear a peace!

2.play: False blood to false blood join'd! gone to be friends!

3.play: Shall Lewis have Blanch, and Blanch those provinces?

4.play: It is not so; thou hast misspoke, misheard:

5.play: Be well advised, tell o'er thy tale again:

6.play: It cannot be; thou dost but say 'tis so:

7.play: I trust I may not trust thee; for thy word

8.play: Is but the vain breath of a common man:

9.play: Believe me, I do not believe thee, man;

10.play: I have a king's oath to the contrary.

11.play: Thou shalt be punish'd for thus frighting me,

12.play: For I am sick and capable of fears,

13.play: Oppress'd with wrongs and therefore full of fears,

14.play: A widow, husbandless, subject to fears,

15.play: A woman, naturally born to fears;

16.play: And though thou now confess thou didst but jest,

17.play: With my vex'd spirits I cannot take a truce,

18.play: But they will quake and tremble all this day.

19.play: What dost thou mean by shaking of thy head?

20.play: Why dost thou look so sadly on my son?

21.play: What means that hand upon that breast of thine?

22.play: Why holds thine eye that lamentable rheum,

23.play: Like a proud river peering o'er his bounds?

24.play: Be these sad signs confirmers of thy words?

25.play: Then speak again; not all thy former tale,

26.play: But this one word, whether thy tale be true.

27.play: SALISBURY: As true as I believe you think them false

28.play: That give you cause to prove my saying true.

29.play: CONSTANCE: O, if thou teach me to believe this sorrow,

30.play: Teach thou this sorrow how to make me die,

31.play: And let belief and life encounter so

32.play: As doth the fury of two desperate men

33.play: Which in the very meeting fall and die.

34.play: Lewis marry Blanch! O boy, then where art thou?

35.play: France friend with England, what becomes of me?

36.play: Fellow, be gone: I cannot brook thy sight:

37.play: This news hath made thee a most ugly man.

38.play: SALISBURY: What other harm have I, good lady, done,

39.play: But spoke the harm that is by others done?

40.play: CONSTANCE: Which harm within itself so heinous is

41.play: As it makes harmful all that speak of it.

42.play: ARTHUR: I do beseech you, madam, be content.

43.play: CONSTANCE: If thou, that bid'st me be content, wert grim,

44.play: Ugly and slanderous to thy mother's womb,

45.play: Full of unpleasing blots and sightless stains,

47.play: Patch'd with foul moles and eye-offending marks,

48.play: I would not care, I then would be content,

49.play: For then I should not love thee, no, nor thou

50.play: Become thy great birth nor deserve a crown.

51.play: But thou art fair, and at thy birth, dear boy,

52.play: Nature and Fortune join'd to make thee great:

53.play: Of Nature's gifts thou mayst with lilies boast,

54.play: And with the half-blown rose. But Fortune, O,

55.play: She is corrupted, changed and won from thee;

56.play: She adulterates hourly with thine uncle John,

57.play: And with her golden hand hath pluck'd on France

58.play: To tread down fair respect of sovereignty,

59.play: And made his majesty the bawd to theirs.

60.play: France is a bawd to Fortune and King John,

61.play: That strumpet Fortune, that usurping John!

62.play: Tell me, thou fellow, is not France forsworn?

63.play: Envenom him with words, or get thee gone

64.play: And leave those woes alone which I alone

67.play: I may not go without you to the kings.

68.play: CONSTANCE: Thou mayst, thou shalt; I will not go with thee:

69.play: I will instruct my sorrows to be proud;

70.play: For grief is proud and makes his owner stoop.

71.play: To me and to the state of my great grief

72.play: Let kings assemble; for my grief's so great

73.play: That no supporter but the huge firm earth

74.play: Can hold it up: here I and sorrows sit;

75.play: Here is my throne, bid kings come bow to it.

76.play: KING PHILIP: 'Tis true, fair daughter; and this blessed day

77.play: Ever in France shall be kept festival:

78.play: To solemnize this day the glorious sun

79.play: Stays in his course and plays the alchemist,

80.play: Turning with splendor of his precious eye

81.play: The meagre cloddy earth to glittering gold:

82.play: The yearly course that brings this day about

83.play: Shall never see it but a holiday.

84.play: A wicked day, and not a holy day!

85.play: What hath this day deserved? what hath it done,

86.play: That it in golden letters should be set

87.play: Among the high tides in the calendar?

88.play: Nay, rather turn this day out of the week,

89.play: This day of shame, oppression, perjury.

90.play: Or, if it must stand still, let wives with child

91.play: Pray that their burthens may not fall this day,

92.play: Lest that their hopes prodigiously be cross'd:

93.play: But on this day let seamen fear no wreck;

94.play: No bargains break that are not this day made:

95.play: This day, all things begun come to ill end,

96.play: Yea, faith itself to hollow falsehood change!

97.play: KING PHILIP: By heaven, lady, you shall have no cause

98.play: To curse the fair proceedings of this day:

99.play: Have I not pawn'd to you my majesty?

100.play: You have beguiled me with a counterfeit

101.play: Resembling majesty, which, being touch'd and tried,

102.play: Proves valueless: you are forsworn, forsworn;

103.play: You came in arms to spill mine enemies' blood,

104.play: But now in arms you strengthen it with yours:

105.play: The grappling vigour and rough frown of war

106.play: Is cold in amity and painted peace,

107.play: And our oppression hath made up this league.

108.play: Arm, arm, you heavens, against these perjured kings!

109.play: A widow cries; be husband to me, heavens!

110.play: Let not the hours of this ungodly day

111.play: Wear out the day in peace; but, ere sunset,

112.play: Set armed discord 'twixt these perjured kings!

115.play: CONSTANCE: War! war! no peace! peace is to me a war

116.play: O Lymoges! O Austria! thou dost shame

117.play: That bloody spoil: thou slave, thou wretch, thou coward!

118.play: Thou little valiant, great in villany!

119.play: Thou ever strong upon the stronger side!

120.play: Thou Fortune's champion that dost never fight

121.play: But when her humorous ladyship is by

122.play: To teach thee safety! thou art perjured too,

123.play: And soothest up greatness. What a fool art thou,

124.play: A ramping fool, to brag and stamp and swear

125.play: Upon my party! Thou cold-blooded slave,

126.play: Hast thou not spoke like thunder on my side,

127.play: Been sworn my soldier, bidding me depend

128.play: Upon thy stars, thy fortune and thy strength,

129.play: And dost thou now fall over to my fores?

130.play: Thou wear a lion's hide! doff it for shame,

131.play: And hang a calf's-skin on those recreant limbs.

132.play: AUSTRIA: O, that a man should speak those words to me!

133.play: BASTARD: And hang a calf's-skin on those recreant limbs.

134.play: AUSTRIA: Thou darest not say so, villain, for thy life.

135.play: BASTARD: And hang a calf's-skin on those recreant limbs.

136.play: KING JOHN: We like not this; thou dost forget thyself.

137.play: KING PHILIP: Here comes the holy legate of the pope.

138.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Hail, you anointed deputies of heaven!

139.play: To thee, King John, my holy errand is.

140.play: I Pandulph, of fair Milan cardinal,

141.play: And from Pope Innocent the legate here,

142.play: Do in his name religiously demand

143.play: Why thou against the church, our holy mother,

144.play: So wilfully dost spurn; and force perforce

146.play: Of Canterbury, from that holy see?

147.play: This, in our foresaid holy father's name,

148.play: Pope Innocent, I do demand of thee.

149.play: KING JOHN: What earthy name to interrogatories

150.play: Can task the free breath of a sacred king?

151.play: Thou canst not, cardinal, devise a name

153.play: To charge me to an answer, as the pope.

154.play: Tell him this tale; and from the mouth of England

155.play: Add thus much more, that no Italian priest

156.play: Shall tithe or toll in our dominions;

157.play: But as we, under heaven, are supreme head,

158.play: So under Him that great supremacy,

159.play: Where we do reign, we will alone uphold,

160.play: Without the assistance of a mortal hand:

161.play: So tell the pope, all reverence set apart

162.play: To him and his usurp'd authority.

163.play: KING PHILIP: Brother of England, you blaspheme in this.

164.play: KING JOHN: Though you and all the kings of Christendom

165.play: Are led so grossly by this meddling priest,

166.play: Dreading the curse that money may buy out;

1.play: LEWIS: My Lord Melun, let this be copied out,

2.play: And keep it safe for our remembrance:

3.play: Return the precedent to these lords again;

4.play: That, having our fair order written down,

5.play: Both they and we, perusing o'er these notes,

6.play: May know wherefore we took the sacrament

7.play: And keep our faiths firm and inviolable.

8.play: SALISBURY: Upon our sides it never shall be broken.

9.play: And, noble Dauphin, albeit we swear

10.play: A voluntary zeal and an unurged faith

11.play: To your proceedings; yet believe me, prince,

12.play: I am not glad that such a sore of time

13.play: Should seek a plaster by contemn'd revolt,

14.play: And heal the inveterate canker of one wound

15.play: By making many. O, it grieves my soul,

16.play: That I must draw this metal from my side

17.play: To be a widow-maker! O, and there

19.play: Cries out upon the name of Salisbury!

20.play: But such is the infection of the time,

21.play: That, for the health and physic of our right,

22.play: We cannot deal but with the very hand

23.play: Of stern injustice and confused wrong.

24.play: And is't not pity, O my grieved friends,

25.play: That we, the sons and children of this isle,

26.play: Were born to see so sad an hour as this;

27.play: Wherein we step after a stranger march

28.play: Upon her gentle bosom, and fill up

29.play: Her enemies' ranks,--I must withdraw and weep

30.play: Upon the spot of this enforced cause,--

31.play: To grace the gentry of a land remote,

33.play: What, here? O nation, that thou couldst remove!

34.play: That Neptune's arms, who clippeth thee about,

35.play: Would bear thee from the knowledge of thyself,

36.play: And grapple thee unto a pagan shore;

37.play: Where these two Christian armies might combine

38.play: The blood of malice in a vein of league,

39.play: And not to spend it so unneighbourly!

40.play: LEWIS: A noble temper dost thou show in this;

41.play: And great affections wrestling in thy bosom

42.play: Doth make an earthquake of nobility.

43.play: O, what a noble combat hast thou fought

44.play: Between compulsion and a brave respect!

45.play: Let me wipe off this honourable dew,

46.play: That silverly doth progress on thy cheeks:

47.play: My heart hath melted at a lady's tears,

49.play: But this effusion of such manly drops,

50.play: This shower, blown up by tempest of the soul,

51.play: Startles mine eyes, and makes me more amazed

52.play: Than had I seen the vaulty top of heaven

53.play: Figured quite o'er with burning meteors.

54.play: Lift up thy brow, renowned Salisbury,

55.play: And with a great heart heave away the storm:

56.play: Commend these waters to those baby eyes

57.play: That never saw the giant world enraged;

58.play: Nor met with fortune other than at feasts,

59.play: Full of warm blood, of mirth, of gossiping.

60.play: Come, come; for thou shalt thrust thy hand as deep

61.play: Into the purse of rich prosperity

62.play: As Lewis himself: so, nobles, shall you all,

63.play: That knit your sinews to the strength of mine.

64.play: And even there, methinks, an angel spake:

65.play: Look, where the holy legate comes apace,

66.play: To give us warrant from the hand of heaven

67.play: And on our actions set the name of right

69.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Hail, noble prince of France!

70.play: The next is this, King John hath reconciled

71.play: Himself to Rome; his spirit is come in,

72.play: That so stood out against the holy church,

73.play: The great metropolis and see of Rome:

74.play: Therefore thy threatening colours now wind up;

75.play: And tame the savage spirit of wild war,

76.play: That like a lion foster'd up at hand,

77.play: It may lie gently at the foot of peace,

78.play: And be no further harmful than in show.

79.play: Your grace shall pardon me, I will not back:

80.play: I am too high-born to be propertied,

81.play: To be a secondary at control,

83.play: To any sovereign state throughout the world.

84.play: Your breath first kindled the dead coal of wars

85.play: Between this chastised kingdom and myself,

86.play: And brought in matter that should feed this fire;

87.play: And now 'tis far too huge to be blown out

88.play: With that same weak wind which enkindled it.

89.play: You taught me how to know the face of right,

90.play: Acquainted me with interest to this land,

91.play: Yea, thrust this enterprise into my heart;

92.play: And come ye now to tell me John hath made

93.play: His peace with Rome? What is that peace to me?

94.play: I, by the honour of my marriage-bed,

95.play: After young Arthur, claim this land for mine;

96.play: And, now it is half-conquer'd, must I back

97.play: Because that John hath made his peace with Rome?

98.play: Am I Rome's slave? What penny hath Rome borne,

99.play: What men provided, what munition sent,

100.play: To underprop this action? Is't not I

101.play: That undergo this charge? who else but I,

102.play: And such as to my claim are liable,

103.play: Sweat in this business and maintain this war?

104.play: Have I not heard these islanders shout out

105.play: 'Vive le roi!' as I have bank'd their towns?

106.play: Have I not here the best cards for the game,

107.play: To win this easy match play'd for a crown?

108.play: And shall I now give o'er the yielded set?

109.play: No, no, on my soul, it never shall be said.

110.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: You look but on the outside of this work.

111.play: Outside or inside, I will not return

112.play: Till my attempt so much be glorified

113.play: As to my ample hope was promised

114.play: Before I drew this gallant head of war,

115.play: And cull'd these fiery spirits from the world,

116.play: To outlook conquest and to win renown

117.play: Even in the jaws of danger and of death.

118.play: What lusty trumpet thus doth summon us?

119.play: BASTARD: According to the fair play of the world,

120.play: Let me have audience; I am sent to speak:

121.play: My holy lord of Milan, from the king

122.play: I come, to learn how you have dealt for him;

123.play: And, as you answer, I do know the scope

124.play: And warrant limited unto my tongue.

125.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: The Dauphin is too wilful-opposite,

126.play: And will not temporize with my entreaties;

127.play: He flatly says he'll not lay down his arms.

128.play: By all the blood that ever fury breathed,

129.play: The youth says well. Now hear our English king;

130.play: For thus his royalty doth speak in me.

131.play: He is prepared, and reason too he should:

133.play: This harness'd masque and unadvised revel,

134.play: This unhair'd sauciness and boyish troops,

135.play: The king doth smile at; and is well prepared

136.play: To whip this dwarfish war, these pigmy arms,

137.play: From out the circle of his territories.

138.play: That hand which had the strength, even at your door,

139.play: To cudgel you and make you take the hatch,

140.play: To dive like buckets in concealed wells,

141.play: To crouch in litter of your stable planks,

142.play: To lie like pawns lock'd up in chests and trunks,

143.play: To hug with swine, to seek sweet safety out

144.play: In vaults and prisons, and to thrill and shake

145.play: Even at the crying of your nation's crow,

146.play: Thinking his voice an armed Englishman;

147.play: Shall that victorious hand be feebled here,

148.play: That in your chambers gave you chastisement?

149.play: No: know the gallant monarch is in arms

150.play: And like an eagle o'er his aery towers,

151.play: To souse annoyance that comes near his nest.

152.play: And you degenerate, you ingrate revolts,

153.play: You bloody Neroes, ripping up the womb

154.play: Of your dear mother England, blush for shame;

155.play: For your own ladies and pale-visaged maids

156.play: Like Amazons come tripping after drums,

157.play: Their thimbles into armed gauntlets change,

158.play: Their needles to lances, and their gentle hearts

160.play: LEWIS: There end thy brave, and turn thy face in peace;

161.play: We grant thou canst outscold us: fare thee well;

162.play: We hold our time too precious to be spent

164.play: CARDINAL PANDULPH: Give me leave to speak.

166.play: LEWIS: We will attend to neither.

167.play: Strike up the drums; and let the tongue of war

168.play: Plead for our interest and our being here.

169.play: BASTARD: Indeed your drums, being beaten, will cry out;

170.play: And so shall you, being beaten: do but start

171.play: An echo with the clamour of thy drum,

172.play: And even at hand a drum is ready braced

173.play: That shall reverberate all as loud as thine;

174.play: Sound but another, and another shall

175.play: As loud as thine rattle the welkin's ear

176.play: And mock the deep-mouth'd thunder: for at hand,

177.play: Not trusting to this halting legate here,

178.play: Whom he hath used rather for sport than need

179.play: Is warlike John; and in his forehead sits

180.play: A bare-ribb'd death, whose office is this day

181.play: To feast upon whole thousands of the French.

182.play: LEWIS: Strike up our drums, to find this danger out.

183.play: BASTARD: And thou shalt find it, Dauphin, do not doubt.
